metadata.summary: Advanced guidelines for writing comprehensive specs for complex, mission-critical features with traceability chains and measurable acceptance criteria.

content.title: Spec Advanced Guidelines
content.summary: Advanced guidelines for writing comprehensive specs for complex, mission-critical features. Full specs have 14 sections: Overview, Problem Statement, Personas, User Stories, Functional Requirements, NFRs, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Assumptions, Dependencies, Related Features, Skills, Open Questions, Appendix. NFRs must be measurable (< 200ms p95, 99.9% uptime). Acceptance criteria must cover happy path + errors + edge cases + performance + security. Every FR must trace to a user story, every AC to an FR.

why.p: Generic specs produce ambiguous tasks that agents interpret inconsistently. Personas prevent "As a user" antipatterns. Measurable NFRs prevent "system should be fast" antipatterns. Out of Scope prevents scope creep from recurring in every planning session. Traceability chains (FR → US → AC) are the primary quality gate for spec completeness.

list.item: Complex features with multiple user types or roles
Features with significant NFRs (performance, security, scalability targets)
Features with external system dependencies or API contracts
Mission-critical features affecting core business metrics
Features requiring cross-role alignment (dev + product + design)
Simple CRUD with no business logic (use minimal spec: Overview + 1-2 stories + basic AC)
Trivial UI copy changes (inline comment sufficient)
Internal refactoring without external behavior change (use design doc only)
Experimental features under development (use lightweight hypothesis spec)

rule.statement: A full spec must include all 14 sections: Overview, Problem Statement, User Personas, User Stories, Functional Requirements, Non-Functional Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Assumptions and Constraints, Dependencies, Related Features, Recommended Skills, Open Questions, Appendix.
rule.rationale: Omitting any section creates a predictable gap: no Personas → generic user stories; no Out of Scope → scope creep in every session; no Assumptions → conflicting implementations.

rule.statement: Problem Statement must answer: Who (target user group), Problem (what they face), Impact (business or user cost), Solution (high-level approach), Success Metric (how to measure the feature succeeded).
rule.rationale: A problem statement without a success metric produces a feature with no clear done criterion. Agents and developers optimize toward undefined targets without it.

rule.statement: Write 2-3 personas with name, role, goals, pain points, and usage context. Reference personas by name in user stories and acceptance criteria.
rule.rationale: Named personas prevent "As a user" stories that apply to nobody specifically. Referencing them in AC forces scenario specificity.

antipattern.description: Persona defined in section 3 but never referenced in user stories or AC.
antipattern.reason: Orphan personas provide no filtering signal. If a persona is not referenced in at least one user story, either the persona is unnecessary or the user stories are too generic.

rule.statement: Every Functional Requirement must trace to at least one User Story (FR-X → US-Y). Every Acceptance Criterion must trace to at least one FR (AC-Z → FR-X). No FR may exist without a US parent. No AC may exist without an FR parent.
rule.rationale: Orphan requirements indicate either gold-plating (FR with no user story) or untestable design (FR with no AC). The chain ensures every requirement serves a stated user need and has a verifiable completion check.

example.code:
### FR-001: Add item to cart Traces to: US-001 (As Sarah, I want to add products to cart with one click)
**AC-001** (validates FR-001): Given Sarah is on a product page, When she clicks "Add to Cart", Then the cart count increments by 1 and a confirmation toast appears.
**AC-002** (validates FR-001): Given the product is out of stock, When Sarah clicks "Add to Cart", Then the button is disabled and shows "Out of Stock".

rule.statement: Every Out of Scope item must include: feature name, one-sentence reason it is excluded, and a "when" (Phase N, v2, never). Do not include items without all three fields.
rule.rationale: Items without explicit exclusion reasoning re-emerge in every planning session. The "when" prevents permanent exclusion from being confused with deferred scope.

rule.statement: Every NFR must specify: quantifiable target, measurement approach (how to measure), and validation method (how to test). NFRs without quantifiable targets must be rejected and rewritten before spec approval.
rule.rationale: "System should be fast" is not an NFR — it is a preference. Without a number, neither developers nor agents can know when the requirement is met.

example.code: ### NFR-001: Add to Cart Latency **Requirement:** Add to cart operation SHALL complete in < 200ms at p95. **Measurement:** Client-side total time from click to UI update (Performance API). **Priority:** Must **Validation:** Load test with 500 concurrent users, measure p95 latency.

antipattern.description: Agent writes NFR as "The system should handle high traffic" or "Response times should be acceptable".
antipattern.reason: LLMs produce qualitative NFR language because they lack knowledge of the specific system's performance profile. Require the agent to propose a specific number and explicitly mark it [estimate] — then the human validates or corrects the target.

rule.statement: For each functional requirement, acceptance criteria must cover: happy path, at least one error case, at least one edge case (boundary condition or empty state). For security-sensitive FRs, add an unauthorized access scenario.
rule.rationale: Specs with only happy path AC produce features that break on first error condition in production. The coverage matrix forces specification of failure behavior before implementation begins.

rule.statement: Use Given-When-Then format for all acceptance criteria. Avoid free-form prose AC. "Works correctly" is not an acceptance criterion.
rule.rationale: Given-When-Then enforces testability. Each field maps directly to a test: Given = test setup, When = action, Then = assertion. Prose AC requires interpretation, causing inconsistent test coverage.

antipattern.description: AC covers only the happy path for all FRs, with no error or edge cases.
antipattern.reason: Agents default to positive-case thinking. Explicitly require error scenarios for every FR that involves user input, external calls, or state transitions. Ask: "What happens when this fails?"

rule.statement: Document every assumption that, if wrong, would require a spec change. Format: "Assumption: [statement]. If wrong: [consequence]."
rule.rationale: Hidden assumptions are the primary source of mid-development spec surprises. Making the "if wrong" consequence explicit accelerates discovery of invalid assumptions during review.

template.purpose: 14-section full spec stub with all required sections
Shell script to verify FR → US → AC coverage
